sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Radiation incidents may involve irradiation, contamination, trauma, or burns. Prehospital priorities are responder safety, time-distance-shielding principles, decontamination support, and ensuring that fear of contamination does not delay necessary lifesaving care.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- No specific acute symptoms from irradiation alone in many cases
- Associated trauma, burns, nausea, vomiting, headache, diarrhoea, or fatigue after significant exposure
- Possible contaminated clothing, dust, or material on the patient
- Scene indicators such as radioactive source signage or industrial/laboratory context

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Do not withhold urgent medical care purely because a radiation sign is present
- Use time, distance, and shielding principles and follow hazardous-material direction
- Washing and controlled clothing removal can greatly reduce contamination spread
- Try not to contaminate vehicles, staff, or hospital areas unnecessarily
- Irradiated patients are not themselves a contamination hazard unless loose radioactive material remains on them

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety and obtain hazardous-material support early
- Remove the patient from the source where safe to do so and follow decontamination instructions from the responsible agency
- Perform primary survey and treat immediately life-threatening problems without unnecessary delay
- Minimise spread of contamination with simple containment measures such as sheeting and controlled handling
- Pre-notify the receiving facility early if contamination is suspected or confirmed

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply ECG or IV access only when clinically needed and avoid unnecessary invasive procedures in contaminated settings
- Provide analgesia, anti-emetic therapy, and supportive resuscitation according to the injury pattern and local protocol
- Continue decontamination support and packaging measures while balancing immediate clinical priorities
- Escalate through hazardous-material, radiation, and hospital coordination pathways according to local system arrangements

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Trauma, burns, or blast injury may be more immediately life threatening than the radiological component itself
- Document suspected source, duration of exposure if known, contamination concerns, and decontamination steps completed before transport
- Transport decisions should reflect the patient's clinical condition as well as contamination-control requirements
